items.q: If one EM wave has a higher frequency than another, what is true about its wavelength?
items.correct: It is shorter — wavelength and frequency are inversely related (c = f x lambda).
items.misconception: It is also longer, because both increase together.
items.random_wrong: It is unchanged — only the speed differs.
items.why: Students treat all wave properties as moving together (more of one = more of the other), missing the fixed-speed constraint that ties them inversely.

items.q: What happens to the current as it goes around a series circuit?
items.correct: It stays the same at every point — current is conserved.
items.misconception: Current gets used up by each component as it flows around the circuit.
items.random_wrong: Current splits in half at the battery so each direction gets some.
items.why: Students confuse current (charge flow) with energy (which is transferred), thinking the bulb 'uses up' current rather than energy.

items.q: What produces a magnetic field around a wire?
items.correct: A moving electric charge — i.e. an electric current.
items.misconception: The metal in the wire is naturally magnetic.
items.random_wrong: The voltage across the wire creates magnetism.
items.why: Students think magnetism is intrinsic to the wire material rather than a consequence of charge motion.
Correct answer: bigger emf. “smaller emf” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: AC. “DC” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Vp/Vs = Np/Ns. Vp=12 V, Np=100, Ns=50. Vs (V)?
items.why: Exact answer: 6. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
